context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: The Question of Ethics

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Development Planners are often faced with having to compromise in reconciling demands from very different groups, and are exposed to (sometimes conflicting) sets of responsibility (e.g. personal, professional, authority). Within this context, what is an ethical compromise? What is the ethics of planners remaining "neutral"? What responsibility should influence their practice? What is the source of ethical values? What ethical framework governs a context of radical cultural pluralism, as in a Western planner working in non-Western countries?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m stuck on a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. it’s about ethical compromise, planner “neutrality,” and how ethics work across diff cultures, and idk how to frame my argument yet.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's start there. Before worrying about framing, what's your gut reaction to this question: can a planner ever truly be 'neutral'? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning of the assignment and hasn't shared any initial thinking yet. Rather than scaffolding heavily, I should ask a simple opening question that activates their prior thinking and gets them to commit to an initial position. This follows the Socratic method and guided discovery without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: my gut says nah, not fully neutral, bc choosing what data matters or who gets heard is already a value choice. but I’m not totally sure how to say that without sounding too absolute.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong starting point. Now push it further: if a planner's choices are always value-laden, whose values are they reflecting? And what does that mean specifically when a Western planner is working in a non-Western context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition and is already thinking critically. They're slightly hesitant about overstating their case, which is actually a good instinct. I should validate the direction of their thinking without endorsing it as complete, and push them to deepen it by connecting it to the specific context of the assignment - development planning across cultures. A single probing question will do.

exchanges.student: i think it could be their own values, their employer’s, the state’s, or donor agendas too. in a non-Western context that gets messy fast, bc a Western planner might act like their standards are “universal” when they’re not.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've identified a real tension. So given that, what do you think a planner should actually do when their professional training pulls one way and the local context pulls another? Is there a principled way to navigate that, or is it just situational?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building well - they've identified multiple sources of competing values and spotted the universalism problem. This maps nicely onto the assignment's questions about cultural pluralism and the source of ethical values. I should push them to think about what follows from this tension practically - what does a planner actually DO with this conflict? This moves toward the 'ethical compromise' and 'ethical framework' parts of the prompt.

exchanges.student: idk, this is where I get stuck tbh. maybe there’s a principle of humility + participation, but I’m not sure if that’s enough when local norms clash w stuff like rights or equality.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're describing - humility vs. universal rights - is actually the heart of the problem. Here's a question: does that tension need to be fully resolved, or could sitting with it productively be part of what ethical practice looks like? What would it mean for a planner to act ethically without having a clean answer?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has hit a genuine philosophical tension - cultural humility vs. universal rights - which is exactly the core of the assignment. They're not stuck on a minor detail, they're stuck on the central dilemma. Rather than resolving it for them, I should ask them to think about whether this tension is resolvable at all, or whether that tension itself is the answer. This is a productive place to be.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, maybe ethical practice isn’t “solving” it but being transparent about tradeoffs and who gets harmed/benefits. like, a planner could justify choices publicly instead of pretending they’re just technical.
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful move - shifting from 'neutrality' to transparency and accountability. Now connect it back to the cultural pluralism piece: does public justification work the same way across cultural contexts, or does that approach itself carry assumptions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely sophisticated idea - ethical practice as public justification and transparency about tradeoffs rather than a clean resolution. This connects well to planning theory (communicative/deliberative planning) and directly addresses the 'ethical compromise' and 'neutrality' questions in the prompt. I should affirm the direction and push them to connect it back to the cultural pluralism question, which they haven't fully resolved yet.

exchanges.student: hmm yeah, even “public justification” sounds kinda liberal-Western rn. maybe it still matters, but the form of accountability prob has to fit local institutions, histories, and ways ppl make decisions.
exchanges.tutor: You've just built a genuinely coherent argument across this conversation. You have a position on neutrality, on competing values, on ethical compromise, and on cultural pluralism. You're close to having a full reflection. Try writing a rough opening paragraph that stakes out your core claim - what's the one thing you want the reader to take away?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now thinking at a sophisticated level - they've self-critiqued their own framework and arrived at a nuanced position: universal accountability matters but its form must be contextually adapted. This is excellent progress and touches on most of the prompt's questions. We've now covered neutrality, competing responsibilities, cultural pluralism, and ethical compromise across about 5 exchanges. I should acknowledge this and push them toward synthesis - they have enough material to start drafting.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe my core claim is: planners can’t be truly neutral, so ethics means being reflexive and accountable about the power behind their choices, esp across cultural difference. can u help me make that opening sound less clunky?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write or revise language for you - that part is yours. But ask yourself: what's the clunky part specifically? Is it word choice, sentence structure, or are you not fully sure what you mean yet? Pinpointing that will help you fix it yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to edit or polish their writing, which I must refuse per the rules. However, I should redirect constructively and help them improve it themselves through questioning rather than doing it for them.

exchanges.student: yeah fair, it’s prob that I’m cramming too much into one sentence. I think I mean: neutrality is a myth, and ethical planning is more about reflexivity, accountability, and humility than pretending to be objective.
exchanges.tutor: That's cleaner and clearer. Now, what are the 2-3 main points you'd use to support that claim in the body of your reflection? Think about the ideas we've discussed.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has naturally simplified and clarified their core claim on their own - this is great progress. I should affirm this briefly and push them toward the body of their reflection, since they have a solid thesis now and need to think about how to structure the rest of the 700-800 words.

exchanges.student: maybe 3 points: first, planners always make value-laden choices; second, “ethical compromise” means negotiating harms openly, not pleasing everyone; third, in non-Western contexts ethics needs humility so universal claims don’t just hide Western power.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid structure. One thing worth checking: the prompt also asks about the *source* of ethical values. Where do your three points currently address that, if at all?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a strong three-point structure that maps well onto the prompt's questions. They've covered neutrality, ethical compromise, and cultural pluralism. We've had about 8 exchanges and they have a thesis and outline. I should affirm this and push them toward drafting, while nudging them to think about one thing they might be missing - the 'source of ethical values' question from the prompt, which hasn't been explicitly addressed.

exchanges.student: oh right, I kinda missed that part. maybe ethical values come from multiple places—professional codes, human rights ideas, personal conscience, and local norms—and the issue is no single source is enough by itself.
exchanges.tutor: You've now addressed every major question in the prompt. You have a thesis, three points, and a fourth idea about the source of values to weave in. It's time to write your draft. Come back when you have something on paper and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now addressed all the key components of the prompt: neutrality, ethical compromise, competing responsibilities, cultural pluralism, and the source of ethical values. We're at exchange 9 and they have a complete outline with a thesis and four supporting ideas. This is a good moment to push them toward actually writing their draft, as continued discussion risks being redundant and they have everything they need.
